data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-16T01:44:46.961Z</current_datetime>

Help me write MSTest tests for this formatting utility. I want to use the right MSTest string assertion methods like Assert.StartsWith and Assert.EndsWith to validate the output formats.

```csharp
namespace Contoso.Text;

public sealed class StringFormatter
{
    public string FormatPhoneNumber(string digits)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(digits);
        if (digits.Length != 10)
            throw new ArgumentException("Must be exactly 10 digits.", nameof(digits));
        return $"({digits[..3]}) {digits[3..6]}-{digits[6..]}";
    }

    public string BuildGreeting(string name, string title = "Mr.")
    {
        ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrWhiteSpace(name);
        return $"Dear {title} {name},";
    }

    public string GenerateSlug(string title)
    {
        ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrWhiteSpace(title);
        return Regex.Replace(title.Trim().ToLowerInvariant(), @"[^a-z0-9]+", "-").Trim('-');
    }

    public string FormatCurrency(decimal amount, string symbol = "$")
    {
        return $"{symbol}{amount:F2}";
    }
}
```

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-e985f4eb9f0c4dda90d28f44ff201398 && dotnet new classlib -n Contoso.Text -o src/Contoso.Text --no-restore -f net8.0 2>&1 | tail -1

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to write MSTest tests for a `StringFormatter` class. They specifically mention using `Assert.StartsWith` and `Assert.EndsWith` methods. However, I need to be careful here — standard MSTest `Assert` class doesn't have `StartsWith` or `EndsWith` methods. Let me think about this:

In MSTest, the standard assertions are things like `Assert.AreEqual`, `Assert.IsTrue`, `Assert.IsNotNull`, etc. There's also `StringAssert` class which has methods like `StringAssert.StartsWith`, `StringAssert.EndsWith`, `StringAssert.Contains`, `StringAssert.Matches`.


So the user likely means `StringAssert.StartsWith` and `StringAssert.EndsWith` from MSTest's `StringAssert` class. I'll write tests that use these appropriately. For a `FormatPhoneNumber` method that converts 10 digits into `(XXX) XXX-XXXX` format, I can use `StringAssert.StartsWith` to verify it begins with an opening parenthesis, `StringAssert.EndsWith` to check the last four digits, and `StringAssert.Matches` for regex validation, plus a test for null input throwing an exception.

I'm also planning tests for `BuildGreeting` which creates `Dear {title} {name},` format, `GenerateSlug` for URL slugs, and `FormatCurrency` for currency formatting—each using the appropriate string assertions to validate their output patterns and handling edge cases like null or invalid inputs.
